danger_signals.visual_form: Mr. X's forces and gang members populating urban environments
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat to player safety; world under criminal control

danger_signals.visual_form: Police and authority figures allied with or controlled by Mr. X
danger_signals.meaning: Authority cannot be trusted; danger comes from institutional sources

danger_signals.visual_form: Multiple hostile NPCs attacking player character
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; world state actively hostile

safety_signals.visual_form: Ex-police vigilantes as protagonists (Axel Stone, Blaze Fielding, Adam Hunter)
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency comes from legitimate authority rejection; safety through resistance

safety_signals.visual_form: Combat moveset and special attacks available to player
safety_signals.meaning: Player empowered to fight back; control and agency present

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Urban Wood Oak City streets as battleground

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Gang members, corrupted officers, syndicate forces
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Crime-controlled zones throughout Wood Oak City

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Police/authority uniforms worn by enemy forces
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Government-controlled spaces now hostile to player

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemies filling streets; urban environment populated with hostile forces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Constant combat encounters; NPCs actively attack player

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Authority figures and police appearing as enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must fight those who should protect; traditional allies become threats
Player agency expressed through combat against system; street-level action

environmental_storytelling.example: Wood Oak City as corrupted American metropolis; urban decay and crime presence communicate institutional failure
Ex-police protagonists immediately establish theme of institutional betrayal and personal justice
Mix of gangsters and corrupted police creates visual narrative of systemic corruption
Side-scrolling movement through crime-controlled territory shows player pushing through syndicate-held space

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engages enemies with special attacks; demonstrates agency and capability
Attack player on sight; aggressive territorial behavior
Fight player same as gang members; visual indicator of institutional corruption
Controls territory through subordinates; absence creates mystery and hierarchy

notes: Streets of Rage communicates world-state primarily through combat encounter density and enemy composition. The visual communication of danger centers on the presence of hostile forces throughout the environment. Political situation (corruption, syndicate control, institutional failure) is communicated through the fact that police/authority figures appear as enemies. The setting of a corrupted American metropolis immediately establishes a world where institutions have failed. Character selection (ex-police vigilantes) frames the narrative as resistance to a corrupted system. This is a Tier T1 analysis based on game description and series context; direct visual asset analysis not available from sources.
